summary: McKenzie reviews Visual Website Optimizer (VWO), a new A/B testing tool from Indian startup Wingify that makes split testing accessible to non-technical small business owners. He explains how founder Paras Chopra pivoted from building an enterprise analytics system to creating an elegantly simple WYSIWYG A/B testing interface after discovering customers needed simplicity, not power. McKenzie praises the product's UI as vastly superior to Google Website Optimizer for non-technical users, while offering candid advice about the startup's Basecamp-inspired design and pricing strategy. He frames VWO as bringing a powerful technique to the 'Fortune 5 Million' — small businesses that couldn't previously access A/B testing.
key_points: VWO's genius is a WYSIWYG interface where users click page elements and type variations, eliminating the HTML/JavaScript knowledge that Google Website Optimizer still requires
Wingify pivoted from enterprise analytics to simple A/B testing after discovering through customer development that small businesses needed pick-up-and-go tools, not powerful systems
Small teams can deliver embarrassingly better UIs than Google because they can focus on a specific user need without enterprise compromises
McKenzie candidly warns the Indian startup about copying Basecamp's design, noting that people don't take copiers seriously and first impressions are hard to shake
The product is potentially worth ten times whatever they plan to charge, since a single additional conversion for a dentist or real estate agent could be worth hundreds or thousands of dollars
money_quotes: They need the Microsoft Word of Internet marketing, a simple pick-up-and-go tool that you can install, employ, and benefit from without needing cooperation from the operations, marketing, and engineering teams which you either a) don't have or b) can't get to do your bidding.
Paras' customers can't use GWO because it isn't nearly non-technical enough.
All the kings horses and all the kings men apparently can't deliver a UI as good as a small team.
People don't take copiers seriously, and it is a hard impression to shake once you've gotten it attached to you, fairly or otherwise.
Trust me, it would be cheap at ten times the price if it worked for dentist offices, real estate agents, car mechanics, and the other constituents of the Fortune 5 Million.
One conversion at the margin is potentially worth hundreds or thousands of dollars, and even a single lead would pay for a month of most software-as-a-service products.
I somehow unwittingly sleepwalked into being that most loathsome of creatures, a technology evangelist.
key_insight: The biggest opportunity in software tooling is making powerful techniques accessible to the millions of small businesses that lack technical teams, not building more powerful features for the technically sophisticated.
